name: Gimbal UFO Full
description: The "Gimbal UFO" refers to a specific unidentified aerial phenomenon (UAP) observed by US Navy pilots in 2015 and subsequently declassified and released to the public. The term originates from the gimbal-mounted targeting pod used to record the video. The "Gimbal" video shows an object rotating in the air, exhibiting no visible means of propulsion. Proponents claim this video, along with other similar UAP observations, represents compelling evidence of technologically advanced craft potentially of extraterrestrial origin, or at least technology far beyond current human capabilities. They argue the object's flight characteristics, such as its apparent ability to hover and rotate without conventional control surfaces, defy conventional explanations. The Pentagon's official stance, as outlined in the ODNI's (Office of the Director of National Intelligence) reports on UAPs, acknowledges the existence of these phenomena but avoids definitive conclusions about their origin. Mainstream scientific and military perspectives offer alternative explanations, including atmospheric phenomena, sensor glitches, misidentification of conventional aircraft, or advanced drone technology, possibly from foreign adversaries. Skeptics emphasize the potential for parallax effects, optical illusions, and limitations in the video's resolution to misrepresent the object's true shape, size, and movement. The lack of corroborating data beyond visual and sensor recordings is also a point of contention. Critiques often highlight the importance of rigorous scientific methodology and the need to rule out mundane explanations before attributing UAP sightings to extraordinary causes.
subtitle: Navy UAP Video and its Implications

claims.claim: The Gimbal video shows a technologically advanced object defying conventional physics.
claims.evidence: The object's apparent rotation and lack of visible propulsion in the Gimbal video.
claims.criticism: The rotation could be an artifact of the camera's gimbal system or an optical illusion. Lack of visible propulsion doesn't preclude known propulsion methods.

claims.claim: The Gimbal UAP is of extraterrestrial origin or represents a foreign adversary's advanced technology.
claims.evidence: The object's observed flight characteristics are unlike any known aircraft.
claims.criticism: Insufficient data to definitively rule out misidentification of known objects, sensor anomalies, or atmospheric phenomena. Extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence.

claims.claim: The US government is covering up the true nature of UAPs like the Gimbal object.
claims.evidence: The initial secrecy surrounding the videos and the lack of definitive explanations from official sources.
claims.criticism: The government's caution is likely due to national security concerns and the need to avoid premature conclusions based on limited data. Gradual declassification suggests transparency.

keyFigures.name: Luis Elizondo
keyFigures.role: Former director of the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) who helped declassify the videos.

keyFigures.name: Mick West
keyFigures.role: Skeptic and debunker who provides alternative explanations for UAP sightings.

timeline.event: US Navy pilots record the Gimbal video.
The Gimbal video is leaked to the public through To The Stars Academy.
The Pentagon officially releases the Gimbal video.
ODNI releases its preliminary assessment on UAPs.

sources.author: Leslie Kean
sources.work: UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record
sources.title: Leslie Kean

sources.author: Mick West
sources.work: Escaping the Rabbit Hole: How to Debunk Conspiracy Theories Using Facts, Logic, and Respect
sources.title: Mick West
